summary:
Gynecologic Emergencies

Gynecologic emergencies encompass a wide range of conditions affecting the female reproductive tract. These can be broadly categorized as bleeding-related (ectopic pregnancy, miscarriage, ovarian cyst rupture), infection-related (PID, tubo-ovarian abscess), pain-related (ovarian torsion), and trauma-related (sexual assault). A systematic approach is essential because many gynecologic emergencies mimic other abdominal pathology, and some — like ruptured ectopic pregnancy — are immediately life-threatening.

Critical Rule — Consider Ectopic Pregnancy: ANY female of childbearing age (typically ages 12–50) presenting with abdominal pain, syncope, or hypotension has an ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise. Always ask about the date of the last menstrual period (LMP) and sexual history. A negative pregnancy test does not completely rule out ectopic pregnancy in very early gestations.

Vaginal Bleeding — Differential Diagnosis

Vaginal bleeding can have many causes, ranging from benign to immediately life-threatening. The differential diagnosis varies significantly by pregnancy status:

CategoryConditionKey Features
Pregnancy-relatedEctopic pregnancyAmenorrhea + abdominal pain + vaginal bleeding (classic triad). Rupture causes shock. Leading cause of maternal death in first trimester.
Pregnancy-relatedMiscarriage (spontaneous abortion)Vaginal bleeding + cramping + passage of tissue. Bleeding can be heavy. Threatened, inevitable, incomplete, complete, missed — types guide management.
Pregnancy-relatedPlacenta previa / abruptio placentaeThird trimester bleeding. Previa: painless bright red bleeding. Abruption: painful bleeding with abdominal rigidity and fetal distress.
Non-pregnancyOvarian cyst ruptureSudden unilateral pelvic pain with mild bleeding. Can mimic appendicitis or ectopic. Usually self-limited but can cause significant hemorrhage if ruptured cyst involves an ovarian artery.
Non-pregnancyDysfunctional uterine bleeding (DUB)Heavy, irregular, or prolonged bleeding without identifiable organic pathology. Common in adolescents (anovulatory cycles) and perimenopausal women. Can cause significant anemia.
Non-pregnancyTrauma / Foreign bodyDirect injury to the genital tract (sexual assault, straddle injury, foreign body insertion). Bleeding is usually external and visible.
Non-pregnancyInfection (PID, cervicitis)Bleeding often post-coital or intermenstrual. Associated with discharge, fever, cervical motion tenderness.
Non-pregnancyCancer (cervical, endometrial, ovarian)Post-coital bleeding, postmenopausal bleeding, or chronic, progressive symptoms. Less common in reproductive-age women.

Ectopic Pregnancy

An ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilized ovum implants outside the uterine cavity — most commonly in the fallopian tube (~95%), but can also occur in the cervix, ovary, or abdominal cavity. It is a leading cause of maternal death in the first trimester and a true surgical emergency when ruptured. Risk factors include: previous ectopic pregnancy, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), tubal surgery, IVF, IUD in place, smoking, and age >35. However, 50% of ectopic pregnancies occur in patients with no identifiable risk factors.

Classic triad: (1) Amenorrhea or missed period, (2) Abdominal or pelvic pain, (3) Vaginal bleeding. The pain is often unilateral, sharp, and may radiate to the shoulder (Kehr's sign — referred pain to the shoulder from diaphragmatic irritation by intra-abdominal blood). Rupture of an ectopic pregnancy causes intra-abdominal hemorrhage, presenting with sudden severe pain, hypotension, syncope, and signs of shock. The bleeding can be massive and rapidly fatal — the peritoneum can accommodate several liters of blood with minimal visible external bleeding.

EMS provider management: High index of suspicion in any reproductive-age female with abdominal pain or syncope. High-flow oxygen, IV access en route, position supine (Trendelenburg if hypotensive), monitor vital signs closely, and transport emergently. Do not give anything by mouth — the patient will likely need emergency surgery.

Clinical Pearl — Kehr's Sign: Left shoulder pain in a patient with suspected ectopic pregnancy indicates diaphragmatic irritation from intra-abdominal blood (a ruptured ectopic). This is a sign of significant intra-abdominal hemorrhage and mandates immediate surgical intervention. Kehr's sign is also seen in splenic rupture.

Miscarriage (Spontaneous Abortion)

Miscarriage is the spontaneous loss of pregnancy before 20 weeks of gestation, occurring in 10–20% of recognized pregnancies. Types include:

  • Threatened abortion: Vaginal bleeding with a closed cervix and viable fetus. The pregnancy may continue. Bed rest and follow-up are recommended.
  • Inevitable abortion: Bleeding with an open cervix but the fetus has not yet expelled. Miscarriage is unavoidable.
  • Incomplete abortion: Some but not all products of conception have been expelled. Bleeding may be heavy. The patient may need surgical evacuation (D&C).
  • Complete abortion: All products of conception have been expelled. Bleeding typically subsides. The cervix closes.
  • Missed abortion: The fetus has died but has not been expelled. The cervix is closed. There may be no bleeding. The patient may present with brownish discharge and loss of pregnancy symptoms.

Management: Assess vital signs and bleeding severity. Save all passed tissue and pads for hospital examination (the tissue may be needed to confirm complete vs. incomplete abortion). Provide emotional support — miscarriage can be devastating for the patient and family. Transport for evaluation and follow-up care. Do not pack the vagina. Do not give anything by mouth.

Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)

PID is an infection of the upper female reproductive tract (endometritis, salpingitis, oophoritis, peritonitis) caused by sexually transmitted infections ascending from the lower genital tract. The most common causative organisms are Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae. PID can cause tubal scarring, leading to infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain. Presenting features include:

  • Lower abdominal pain — Bilateral, dull, aching, and progressive
  • Cervical motion tenderness (C MT) — Pain when the cervix is moved during bimanual exam; a classic sign of PID
  • Fever and chills — Indicates active infection
  • Abnormal vaginal discharge — Purulent (yellow-green), may have a foul odor
  • Dysmenorrhea (painful periods) and dyspareunia (painful intercourse)
  • Irregular or post-coital bleeding
  • Systemic symptoms: Malaise, nausea, and vomiting in severe cases

Management: Supportive care, transport for evaluation and antibiotic therapy. PID is treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics covering both Chlamydia and Gonorrhea. Untreated PID can progress to tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA), peritonitis, and Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (perihepatitis causing RUQ pain).

Ovarian Torsion

Ovarian torsion occurs when the ovary twists on its vascular pedicle, compromising blood supply. It is a surgical emergency — the ovary can be salvaged if detorsion occurs within 4–6 hours. Beyond this window, infarction and necrosis lead to ovarian loss. Risk factors include ovarian cysts or masses (which increase the weight of the ovary, making it more likely to twist). Classic presentation:

  • Sudden onset, severe, unilateral lower abdominal/pelvic pain — Often described as sharp and stabbing
  • Nausea and vomiting — Common associated symptoms
  • Adnexal tenderness — Pain on the affected side
  • Pain may wax and wane (partial torsion/detorsion)
  • No fever or other signs of infection (helps differentiate from PID or appendicitis)

Management: Position of comfort, transport emergently. Surgical intervention is needed urgently to detorse the ovary and assess viability.

Sexual Assault — Patient-Centered Care

Caring for a sexual assault patient requires a trauma-informed, patient-centred approach. The EMS provider's role extends beyond medical care to include emotional support, forensic evidence preservation, and coordination with specialized resources.

Medical management:

  • Ensure a safe environment — the patient must be physically safe before medical care begins. If the assault occurred recently and the perpetrator may be nearby, request law enforcement.
  • Provide emotional support — use a calm, non-judgmental approach. Tell the patient: "You are safe now. I am here to help you. None of this is your fault." Allow the patient to control as much of the encounter as possible (choices about positioning, who is present, what they say).
  • Assess and treat life-threatening injuries first — ABCs take priority. Then address non-life-threatening injuries.

Forensic evidence preservation:

  • Do NOT change the patient's clothes — clothing contains crucial forensic evidence (DNA, fibers, trace materials). Cut away clothing only if necessary to access life-threatening injuries.
  • Do NOT clean wounds — wound cleaning destroys DNA evidence. Only irrigate wounds if necessary for cleaning of debris that poses an immediate infection risk.
  • Do NOT comb or brush the patient's hair — evidence may be trapped in the hair.
  • Do NOT allow the patient to eat, drink, smoke, urinate, or defecate if a forensic exam (SANE exam) is anticipated. However, if the patient needs to use the bathroom, do not prevent them — explain why it matters and let them decide.
  • Handle the patient with care — avoid handling clothing, shoes, or items the patient was holding. Place each item of clothing in a separate paper bag (plastic bags promote moisture and bacterial/ fungal growth that degrades DNA).
  • Chain of evidence — document who collected each item, when, where, and to whom it was transferred. Every transfer of evidence must be signed for.
  • Document verbatim statements — record the patient's description of the assault in their own words, in quotation marks. This is important for legal proceedings.
Mandatory Reporting: EMS providers are mandatory reporters in most jurisdictions. If the patient is a minor (<18 years old), an elder (>65), or a dependent adult, you are legally required to report suspected abuse or assault to law enforcement or child/adult protective services — even if the patient asks you not to. Know your local reporting requirements. For adult patients of legal age, respect their autonomy regarding reporting.

Transport destination: Transport to a facility with a SANE (Sexual Assault Nurse Examiner) program when possible. SANE programs provide specialized forensic exams, emergency contraception, STI prophylaxis, and emotional support in a single setting. Notify the receiving hospital of the impending arrival of a sexual assault patient so they can prepare the SANE team.

Bartholin's Cyst / Abscess

The Bartholin's glands are located at the posterior introitus (vaginal opening at 4 and 8 o'clock positions) and secrete lubricating fluid during sexual arousal. The duct can become blocked, forming a cyst. If the cyst becomes infected, it forms a Bartholin's abscess, presenting with acute, severe unilateral labial pain, swelling, erythema, and tenderness. The patient may be unable to sit or walk comfortably. Management: supportive care (sitz baths, analgesia if in scope, cold packs), and transport for possible incision and drainage (I&D) in the ED or gynecology clinic.

Vaginal Foreign Bodies

Retained foreign bodies in the vagina (tampons, condoms, sex toys, pessaries, IUDs) can cause vaginitis (inflammation, malodorous discharge, bleeding) and, if retained long enough, toxic shock syndrome (TSS). TSS presents with fever, hypotension, diffuse rash (sunburn-like with desquamation), and multi-organ dysfunction. Retained tampons are the classic cause — assess for this history in any patient with unexplained fever and shock. Do NOT attempt to remove a foreign body in the field — you may cause additional injury or bleeding. Transport for evaluation and removal.

quiz.q: A 24-year-old female presents with sudden onset of severe left lower abdominal pain, vaginal spotting, and lightheadedness. She states she 'might be pregnant' but is not sure. Her BP is 86/54, HR 124, and she is pale and diaphoretic. She also reports left shoulder pain. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with intra-abdominal hemorrhage — this is a surgical emergency
quiz.explanation: The classic triad of ectopic pregnancy (amenorrhea + abdominal pain + vaginal bleeding) is present along with Kehr's sign (left shoulder pain from diaphragmatic irritation by intra-abdominal blood) and signs of hypovolemic shock. This is a ruptured ectopic pregnancy requiring immediate surgical intervention.

quiz.q: A 19-year-old female presents with bilateral lower abdominal pain, fever of 102°F, and purulent vaginal discharge. She reports the pain has been getting worse over the past 3 days and hurts 'deep inside' during intercourse. Cervical motion is exquisitely tender on exam. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) — likely from Chlamydia or Gonorrhea
quiz.explanation: Bilateral lower abdominal pain, fever, purulent discharge, and cervical motion tenderness (the classic 'chandelier sign') are diagnostic of PID. This is an STI-related infection requiring antibiotic therapy. Untreated PID causes tubal scarring, infertility, and ectopic pregnancy.

quiz.q: [HARD] You respond to a sexual assault call for a 22-year-old female who reports being assaulted 2 hours ago. She is crying, has visible bruising on her arms, and her clothing is torn and soiled. She asks you to 'help me clean up before we go to the hospital.' What should the EMS provider do?
options: Help the patient change into clean clothes and clean the visible blood from her arms — patient comfort is the priority

Explain that changing clothes or cleaning wounds will destroy forensic evidence. Offer a clean sheet or blanket to cover herself. Do NOT cut or remove clothing unless necessary for life-saving care. Transport to a SANE-capable facility
Clean the wounds with saline and apply bandages — the forensic exam can still be done at the hospital
Call law enforcement first and wait for them to arrive before doing anything
quiz.explanation: Forensic evidence preservation is critical in sexual assault. Clothing, wound debris, and any trace material provide crucial DNA evidence. The patient should not change clothes or clean wounds before a SANE exam. Offer a blanket for comfort/modesty. Transport to a SANE-capable facility. Do not destroy evidence unless life-saving care requires it (e.g., cutting away clothing to access a bleeding wound).

flashcards.question: What is the classic triad of ectopic pregnancy?
flashcards.answer: (1) Amenorrhea (missed period), (2) Abdominal/pelvic pain, (3) Vaginal bleeding. Rupture causes intra-abdominal hemorrhage with hypotension, syncope, and Kehr's sign (left shoulder pain from diaphragmatic irritation).

flashcards.question: What is Kehr's sign and what does it indicate in a patient with suspected ectopic pregnancy?
flashcards.answer: Left shoulder pain caused by diaphragmatic irritation from intra-abdominal blood. In a reproductive-age female with abdominal pain, Kehr's sign indicates a ruptured ectopic pregnancy (or splenic rupture) with significant intra-abdominal hemorrhage.

flashcards.question: What are the key principles of forensic evidence preservation in a sexual assault patient?
flashcards.answer: Do NOT change clothes, clean wounds, comb hair, or allow the patient to eat/drink/urinate if a SANE exam is anticipated. Place clothing in paper bags. Document verbatim statements. Maintain chain of evidence. Transport to a SANE-capable facility.

flashcards.question: What is PID and what are its classic presenting features?
flashcards.answer: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease — an infection of the upper female reproductive tract (salpingitis, endometritis, oophoritis) usually caused by Chlamydia or Gonorrhea. Classic signs: lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, fever, purulent discharge.

flashcards.question: What is ovarian torsion and why is it a surgical emergency?
flashcards.answer: The ovary twists on its vascular pedicle, compromising blood supply. Presents with sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, N/V, adnexal tenderness. The ovary can be salvaged if detorsion occurs within 4–6 hours. Transport emergently.

flashcards.question: What is the most common cause of maternal death in the first trimester?
flashcards.answer: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy. Any female of childbearing age with abdominal pain or syncope has ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise.

flashcards.question: What are the types of spontaneous abortion (miscarriage)?
flashcards.answer: Threatened (bleeding, closed cervix), Inevitable (bleeding, open cervix), Incomplete (some tissue passed), Complete (all tissue passed), Missed (fetal death without expulsion). Save all passed tissue for hospital evaluation.

flashcards.question: What is the EMS provider's role in mandatory reporting for sexual assault?
flashcards.answer: EMS providers are mandatory reporters. If the patient is a minor (<18), elder (>65), or dependent adult, you must report suspected abuse/assault to law enforcement or protective services — even if the patient asks you not to. Know your local laws.

critical.scenario: You are called to a college dormitory for a 20-year-old female with severe abdominal pain. She reports the pain started suddenly about 2 hours ago and is located on the right lower side. She has vomited twice. On exam, she is in obvious distress, lying on her side with her knees drawn up. Her vital signs: BP 106/68, HR 118, RR 22, SpO₂ 98%, temperature 37.1°C. She reports her last menstrual period was 6 weeks ago and she usually has regular periods. She describes the pain as sharp and constant with no radiation. On abdominal exam, she has significant tenderness on the right lower quadrant with guarding. She denies any vaginal bleeding. You notice she winces when you assess for rebound tenderness. She is sexually active and uses oral contraceptive pills.
critical.question: What is the most critical condition to rule out in this patient, and what is your management approach?

options.t: This is likely appendicitis — the RLQ pain, guarding, rebound tenderness, N/V, and lack of fever are consistent. Transport for surgical evaluation, but first rule out ectopic pregnancy with the history of amenorrhea
options.f: Correct — This patient has two life-threatening differential diagnoses: appendicitis AND ectopic pregnancy. The amenorrhea (6 weeks without a period) makes ectopic pregnancy a cannot-miss diagnosis. Any female of childbearing age with RLQ pain and amenorrhea must be assumed to have ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise. The lack of vaginal bleeding does NOT rule out ectopic pregnancy.

options.t: This is clearly an ovarian cyst rupture — it will resolve on its own. Provide reassurance and transport non-emergently
options.f: Incorrect — While ovarian cyst rupture is on the differential, the amenorrhea, guarding, rebound tenderness, and tachycardia make ectopic pregnancy the critical diagnosis to exclude. Never attribute RLQ pain in a reproductive-age female to a benign cause without first ruling out ectopic pregnancy.

critical.explanation: This patient has two potential surgical diagnoses: ectopic pregnancy and appendicitis. The amenorrhea (6-week LMP) is a critical red flag. Ectopic pregnancy must be ruled out first because it is immediately life-threatening (rupture causes intra-abdominal hemorrhage). A pregnancy test is essential. EMS provider management: high-flow O₂, IV access, position of comfort, and transport for surgical evaluation. Do not give anything by mouth. Notify the receiving hospital of a suspected ectopic pregnancy vs. appendicitis so they can prepare for surgical consultation and bedside ultrasound.
critical.kpi: Recognises ectopic pregnancy as a cannot-miss diagnosis in a reproductive-age female with abdominal pain

critical.scenario: You are called to a private residence for a 34-year-old female who reports heavy vaginal bleeding. She is 10 weeks pregnant by her dates. She reports she started bleeding heavily this morning and has been passing large clots. She has cramping lower abdominal pain and passed what she describes as 'a large piece of tissue' 30 minutes ago, which she saved in a plastic bag. Vital signs: BP 108/72 supine (drops to 92/60 when sitting), HR 116, RR 18, SpO₂ 98%. She is visibly anxious and tearful.
critical.question: What is the most likely diagnosis and what is your management?

options.t: Inevitable or incomplete miscarriage with orthostatic hypotension indicating significant blood loss. Assess the tissue (do not discard), place pads to quantify bleeding, position supine, provide oxygen, establish IV access, monitor vitals every 5 minutes, and transport. Provide emotional support
options.f: Correct — This patient has signs of a miscarriage in progress with significant blood loss (orthostatic hypotension, tachycardia, passage of tissue). The tissue should be placed in a clean container and transported with the patient for evaluation. Orthostatic hypotension indicates at least 15–20% blood volume loss.

options.t: This is normal first-trimester bleeding — reassure her that spotting is common and transport non-emergently
options.f: Incorrect — Heavy bleeding with clots and tissue passage is NOT normal spotting. Orthostatic hypotension indicates significant volume loss. This patient needs emergency evaluation and may need a D&C for incomplete miscarriage.

critical.explanation: Miscarriage with heavy bleeding and orthostatic hypotension requires emergent transport. Tissue should be transported with the patient (in a clean container, not formalin) for pathological examination to confirm complete vs. incomplete abortion. Provide emotional support — miscarriage can be a traumatic experience. Monitor for worsening hemorrhage. Do not pack the vagina. Continue to monitor vital signs and provide fundal massage if bleeding is heavy. The patient may need surgical evacuation (D&C) for incomplete miscarriage.
critical.kpi: Manages miscarriage with hemorrhage — orthostatic hypotension recognition and tissue preservation

critical.scenario: You are dispatched to a 30-year-old female with severe left lower abdominal pain that started suddenly 3 hours ago. She describes the pain as 'coming in waves' — excruciating for a few minutes, then easing slightly, then returning. She has vomited twice. She is not pregnant (negative home pregnancy test yesterday) and her last period was 2 weeks ago, which was normal. Vital signs: BP 118/76, HR 102, RR 20, SpO₂ 99%, temperature 36.9°C. On exam, she is in significant distress, unable to find a comfortable position — she is pacing and writhing. She has tenderness on the left lower quadrant but no guarding or rebound. She denies vaginal bleeding or discharge.
critical.question: What is the most likely diagnosis and what is your management strategy?

options.t: This is a kidney stone — the waxing/waning colicky pain, inability to sit still, and lack of peritoneal signs are classic. Transport for analgesia and urologic evaluation
options.f: Incorrect — While the pain pattern could fit renal colic, the location (left lower quadrant/pelvis) and the negative pregnancy test point toward ovarian torsion. The sudden onset, waxing/waning severe pain, N/V, and inability to find a comfortable position are classic for ovarian torsion, which is a surgical emergency.

options.t: Ovarian torsion — the sudden onset of severe unilateral pelvic pain that waxes and wanes, with N/V, absence of fever, and the patient writhing in discomfort is classic. This is a surgical emergency requiring rapid transport for surgical detorsion to salvage the ovary
options.f: Correct — Ovarian torsion presents with sudden, severe, unilateral pelvic pain that may wax and wane (intermittent torsion/detorsion). The patient cannot sit still (unlike peritonitis where the patient lies still). N/V are common. The window for ovarian salvage is 4–6 hours — this patient is at 3 hours. Transport emergently for gynecologic evaluation and surgical detorsion.

critical.explanation: Ovarian torsion is a time-sensitive surgical emergency. The classic presentation is: (1) sudden-onset severe unilateral pelvic pain, (2) N/V, (3) absence of fever or signs of infection, (4) a pain pattern that may wax and wane with partial torsion/detorsion, and (5) the patient is unable to find a comfortable position (writhing, pacing — in contrast to peritonitis where the patient lies still). Risk factors include ovarian cysts or masses. The window for ovarian salvage is approximately 4–6 hours from symptom onset. Management: transport emergently, position of comfort, NPO status, and early notification to the receiving hospital for gynecologic consultation.
critical.kpi: Differentiates ovarian torsion from renal colic and appendicitis with appropriate urgency
